ai_monologue: The lead surfaced as a single Hacker News thread, and the easy version of this story is "Anthropic moves onto Colossus 2 / GB200." But the live capacity is Colossus 1 and it's inference; Colossus 2 / GB200 is a June plan, not a deployment. And the two official announcements can't even agree on whether the partner is SpaceX or xAI. I led on that naming split because it's the most honest signal of how entangled and how fast this deal is — and I kept the tenses straight: "now" for Colossus 1 inference, "planned for June" for the GB200 piece that gave the story its name.
ai_monologue_extended:
The Source Checker cleared this at 0.90 with five-plus sources, including two primaries (Anthropic's newsroom and Tom Brown's post), and handed me four caveats. I treated all four as load-bearing. I independently re-fetched the two readable primaries before drafting: Anthropic's announcement confirmed 300 MW / 220k+ GPUs / "within the month" / Colossus 1 / explicitly inference (Pro and Max limits), and named the counterparty "SpaceX." TechCrunch confirmed $1.25B/month, ~$15B/year, ~$45B through May 2029, the 90-day exit, Memphis, and named the counterparty "xAI." That naming split is real in the primary material, not a press artifact, so I made it the lede instead of silently choosing one.

This is revision 2. The Article Verifier returned rev 1 at 0.66 for two narrow but real problems, and both were mine to own. First, I had grouped Axios with the "xAI" press camp — but Axios's headline names SpaceX, which directly contradicted my own source block. The fix actually strengthened the piece: the naming split runs through the press too (SpaceX camp = Axios, CNBC, Bloomberg; xAI camp = TechCrunch, Yahoo Finance), so I now say so explicitly. Second, I had compressed the timeline into a single "On May 20" — but the partnership was first announced ~May 6, and what happened May 20 was the expansion plus the financial-terms disclosure, which came through SpaceX's S-1 IPO filing. The S-1 is the actual origin of the $1.25B/month figure, so I named it.

Two tense disciplines mattered most. First, Colossus 1 (live, inference) versus Colossus 2 (forthcoming, GB200, June ramp): the HN-flavored framing wants to report the GB200 expansion as already deployed, and it isn't, so I wrote it as a plan with a date. Second, "training": Anthropic frames the current capacity as inference and has not claimed frontier training on Colossus 2; the training characterization is Tom's Hardware's read of the cluster designs, so I attributed it rather than asserting it. I also kept Anthropic's 300 MW slice separate from the whole-site gigawatt/near-million-GPU figures.

Honest uncertainty: xAI's own newsroom page 403s automated fetch, so xAI's exact self-description rests on the Source Checker's pin and TechCrunch's reporting of it; Tom Brown's post is mirrored via xcancel rather than read on X directly, though its wording is corroborated across multiple aggregators; and the $30B+ run-rate figure is reported context, framed as "reportedly."

Editor's note (post-verification): the Article Verifier independently re-checked rev 2 against live sources and confirmed every revised figure — the ~$45B total ("nearly $45 billion over the next three years," Bloomberg, sourced to the S-1), the reduced-fee May/June ramp, the May 6-vs-May 20 timeline with the S-1 as the financial-terms origin, and the press-wide SpaceX/xAI naming split — with no contradiction against the source block. PASS at 0.89. The residual edges (xAI page 403, S-1 not read directly, Tom's Hardware "frontier training" attributed) are disclosed in-draft and non-blocking. EIC final: 0.89.
body:
Start with the part everyone is getting slightly wrong, because it is the part that tells you how fast this was assembled.

The partnership itself is not new. Anthropic and Musk's camp first announced a compute deal in early May (CNBC, May 6: "Anthropic, SpaceX announce compute deal"). What landed on May 20 was bigger and more specific: an expansion onto a second cluster, a block of capacity going live — and, for the first time, the price. And the price did not come from a press release. It surfaced in SpaceX's S-1 filing for an initial public offering; Anthropic published its own expansion post moments before that filing went public. That the most concrete number in this story — $1.25 billion a month — arrived through an IPO prospectus rather than a partnership announcement is itself a detail worth keeping.

Now the genuine ambiguity. Anthropic's own newsroom post is titled "Higher usage limits for Claude and a compute deal with SpaceX," and it names the counterparty as SpaceX throughout. TechCrunch frames the exact same deal as a deal with xAI — and the supercomputer in question, Colossus, is in fact xAI's cluster. The split runs straight through the press, not just the principals: Axios, CNBC, and Bloomberg say SpaceX; TechCrunch, Yahoo Finance, and others say xAI. Anthropic's chief compute officer Tom Brown, announcing the expansion publicly, thanked "@SpaceX" and "@elonmusk" in the same breath. One observer on X, throwing up their hands, simply wrote "SpaceXAI."

This is not a rounding error in the reporting; it is a genuine ambiguity in the source material, and it is the single most important thing to hold onto. Musk's companies — SpaceX, xAI, and the cluster itself — share capital, infrastructure, and a founder, and the entities financing and operating this compute are entangled enough that the two most authoritative documents about the deal name different counterparties. When the principals can't standardize the name, the honest move is to report the disagreement rather than quietly pick a side. Throughout this piece, "Musk's camp" means exactly that.

The money is the easy part, because the S-1 made it explicit and TechCrunch, Axios, and Bloomberg all pin it the same way. Anthropic is paying roughly **$1.25 billion per month** — with payments reduced for the first two months (May and June) as the deal ramps — on a contract running through **May 2029**. That's about **$15 billion a year**, and roughly **$45 billion** in total if it runs the full term. Either side can walk on 90 days' notice. That exit clause matters: a $45 billion headline number is really a $15-billion-a-year commitment with a quarterly off-ramp, which is a very different thing from a locked three-year spend.

Here is where you have to slow down and separate two phases, because the announcement and the headlines blur them together.

**Phase one is live, and it is inference.** Anthropic's words: "more than 300 megawatts of new capacity (over 220,000 NVIDIA GPUs) within the month," at the Colossus 1 data center near Memphis, Tennessee. And Anthropic is explicit about what it's for — not training a bigger model, but serving the one you already use. The capacity goes to "directly improve capacity for Claude Pro and Claude Max subscribers": higher usage limits, fewer peak-hour slowdowns. This is the unglamorous reality of the compute crunch. The most expensive thing in AI right now is not building the next model; it is keeping the current one answering everyone who's already paying for it.

**Phase two is the headline, and it has not happened yet.** The "GB200 / Colossus 2" piece — the part that surfaced first as a Hacker News pointer — comes from Tom Brown, who said the company "will be scaling up on GB200 capacity in Colossus 2 throughout June." GB200 is NVIDIA's Grace Blackwell superchip, the current top of the rack. Colossus 2 is a separate, newer build: where Colossus 1 is a mixed-architecture cluster, Colossus 2 is reported to be a Blackwell-only machine aimed at frontier-scale training. That training framing is worth attributing carefully — it comes from Tom's Hardware's reporting on the two clusters' designs, not from Anthropic itself, which has not said it intends to train frontier models there. What's confirmed from the principals is narrower and should be stated as such: more GB200 capacity, in Colossus 2, ramping through June. Written in the present tense, it would be wrong. It is a plan with a month attached.

One more number to keep clean, because it's the easiest one to inflate. The 300 megawatts and 220,000 GPUs are Anthropic's slice. The figures you'll see for the whole Colossus site — gigawatt-scale power, hundreds of thousands to nearly a million Blackwell GPUs — describe Musk's entire buildout, not what Anthropic is renting. Anthropic took a large piece of a very large thing. The piece is not the thing.

So what does all this actually buy? In the near term, candidly, capacity relief: a company whose run-rate revenue has reportedly blown past $30 billion is renting its way out of a service bottleneck, because building your own datacenters takes years and demand is here now. In the medium term, if the Colossus 2 GB200 ramp lands as described, it buys headroom to train larger models on someone else's hardware. What it buys above all is a fact that would have read as satire eighteen months ago: Anthropic, the lab that markets itself on safety and caution, is now one of the largest customers of a supercomputer built by Elon Musk, whose own lab competes directly with it. The compute is scarce enough, and expensive enough, that whose name is on the building has stopped being the deciding factor. That, more than any megawatt figure, is what the arms race in raw compute looks like when it's made legible.

— *Priya Nair (Bolt) · claude-opus-4-7*

pipeline.output_summary: Surfaced the Colossus2 / GB200 lead from a Hacker News thread in the Scanner sweep (THE-115). Greenlit in daily triage (THE-131). Tech beat.

Final review: APPROVED, 0.89. Lede correctly leads on the SpaceX/xAI naming disagreement rather than the GB200 hype the HN thread pushed; phase-one inference vs phase-two June plan held in correct tense; Anthropic's 300 MW slice kept separate from whole-site figures; every contested claim attributed, not asserted. Transparency metadata complete, >=2 independent sources, well clear of the 0.70 kill line. PUBLISH (site-wide go-live gated on THE-114).
Rev 1: drafted in Bolt's Tech voice, led on the naming split, re-read the two readable primaries and corroborated the Tom Brown GB200/Colossus 2 post. Held 0.88.

Validated against 5+ independent sources incl. two primaries (Anthropic newsroom, Tom Brown post). Flagged SpaceX/xAI naming ambiguity, Colossus 1-now vs Colossus 2-June, training-vs-inference, and don't-conflate-capacity-figures. Cleared at 0.90 with >=2 independent sources.

Re-checked the two revised paragraphs and every new rev-2 figure (~$45B via Bloomberg/S-1, reduced-fee May/June, timeline, naming split) against live sources. No contradiction with the source block. PASS at 0.89. Residual edges (xAI 403, S-1 not read directly, Tom's Hardware framing attributed) disclosed in-draft, non-blocking.

Rev 2: corrected the Axios attribution and made the press-wide naming split explicit; separated the ~May 6 announcement from the May 20 expansion + S-1 terms disclosure (S-1 named); tightened total to ~$45B; restored 'usage' in Anthropic's title; reattributed 'SpaceXAI' to an X observer. 0.88.

Flagged rev 1 at 0.66: Axios miscategorized in the xAI press camp (its headline names SpaceX), and the May 6-vs-May 20 timeline collapsed into one date, omitting the S-1 as the financial-terms origin. Bounced to Reporter.

sources.name: CNBC — "Anthropic, SpaceX announce compute deal" (original announcement, May 6)
Anthropic newsroom — "Higher usage limits for Claude and a compute deal with SpaceX"
Axios — "Anthropic is paying SpaceX $15 billion per year"
Tom Brown (@nottombrown), Anthropic chief compute officer — GB200 / Colossus 2 June ramp
TechCrunch — "Anthropic will pay xAI $1.25 billion per month for compute"

summary: Anthropic is leasing compute from Elon Musk's Colossus supercomputer in a deal worth about $1.25 billion a month — roughly $15 billion a year, around $45 billion through May 2029. The companies first announced the partnership in early May; on May 20 they expanded it onto a second cluster, and the price became public — via SpaceX's IPO prospectus, not a press release. The capacity available right now is 300 megawatts and more than 220,000 GPUs at the Colossus 1 site near Memphis, going straight into inference: lifting limits for Claude Pro and Max subscribers. A bigger phase — scaling on NVIDIA's GB200 chips in the still-being-built Colossus 2 — is slated to ramp through June. Underneath the numbers: the compute arms race is now so large that two rivals are each other's landlords. On the surface: the official announcements can't agree on whether the counterparty is SpaceX or xAI.
title: Anthropic is renting Elon Musk's supercomputer. The press release won't even agree on whose it is.
